verses.commentary: Jesus crossed the Sea of Galilee (also called Tiberias). Movement initiates new revelation. Geography sets the stage for theological demonstration.
A great crowd followed because they saw the signs He performed on the sick. Sign-based following. They seek benefits, not the Benefactor. Surface attraction.
Jesus went up on a mountainside and sat down with His disciples. Moses on Sinai echoes. Mountain teaching, new law-giver. Jesus as the new Moses.
The Passover was near. John marks time deliberately. Passover context frames what follows. Exodus deliverance remembered, new deliverance foreshadowed.
Where shall we buy bread for these people? Jesus tests Philip. The question reveals Philip's limited vision. Human calculation versus divine provision.
He asked this only to test him, for He already had in mind what He was going to do. Jesus knows His plan. The test exposes disciples' faith, not informs Jesus.
It would take more than half a year's wages. Philip calculates impossibility. Economics declare inadequacy. Human resources cannot meet divine-scale needs.
Andrew, Simon Peter's brother, spoke up. Tentative offer. He sees a resource but doubts its sufficiency.
A boy with five barley loaves and two small fish. How far will they go among so many? Barley bread, food of the poor. Inadequate resource offered. Faith mixed with doubt.
Have the people sit down. About five thousand men. Jesus organizes before providing. Order precedes miracle. The number suggests twelve thousand total with women and children.
Jesus took the loaves, gave thanks, distributed as much as they wanted. Eucharistic language. Creation from minimal resources. Abundance from scarcity. God satisfies fully.
Gather the pieces that are left over. Let nothing be wasted. Stewardship in abundance. God's provision demands careful use. Excess is not excuse for carelessness.
They filled twelve baskets. One basket per tribe of Israel. Symbolic abundance. The fragments exceed the original supply. God multiplies beyond need.
Surely this is the Prophet who is to come into the world. Deuteronomy 18:15. They recognize the sign. Moses gave manna; Jesus gives bread. Messianic expectations stirred.
Knowing they intended to make Him king by force, He withdrew to a mountain by Himself. Jesus refuses coerced kingship. His kingdom comes on His terms, His timing. Political messianism rejected.
When evening came, His disciples went down to the lake. Transition to night. Darkness and danger ahead. Disciples without Jesus, vulnerable.
They got into a boat and set off for Capernaum. It was dark, and Jesus had not yet joined them. Literal and spiritual darkness. Absence creates fear. Separation from Jesus brings insecurity.
A strong wind was blowing and the waters grew rough. Storm threatens. Chaos erupts. Nature's hostility mirrors spiritual danger.
They had rowed three or four miles. They saw Jesus approaching, walking on the water. They were frightened. Jesus masters nature. Divine power over creation. Fear precedes recognition.
It is I; don't be afraid. Egō eimi (ἐγώ εἰμι), I AM. Divine self-identification. Yahweh present on the water. Fear yields to revelation.
They were willing to take Him into the boat, and immediately the boat reached the shore. Another miracle embedded. Time and space collapse. Presence of Jesus brings instant arrival.
The crowd realized only one boat had been there, and Jesus had not entered it with His disciples. They deduce impossibility. How did Jesus cross? The question drives seeking.
Boats from Tiberias landed near where people had eaten the bread after the Lord gave thanks. Persistent pursuit. They track Jesus. Surface motives.
The crowd got into boats and went to Capernaum in search of Jesus. Energetic seeking. But for what? Bread or Bread-giver?
Rabbi, when did You get here? Innocent question hides deeper ignorance. They ask logistics, miss theology.
You are looking for Me, not because you saw the signs but because you ate the loaves and had your fill. Jesus exposes motives. Belly-driven religion. They want benefits, not relationship.

Do not work for food that spoils, but for food that endures to eternal life, which the Son of Man will give you. Shift from physical to spiritual. Temporary versus eternal. Jesus offers lasting satisfaction.
What must we do to do the works God requires? Work-based mindset persists. They think performance earns salvation. Old covenant thinking.
The work of God is this: to believe in the one He has sent. Faith is the work. Trust, not achievement. Believing Jesus is the singular divine requirement.
What sign then will You give that we may see it and believe You? Astonishing. They just witnessed the feeding. Insatiable demand for proof. Unbelief always wants more evidence.
Our ancestors ate the manna in the wilderness. They compare Jesus to Moses. Manna from heaven set the standard. Can Jesus match it?
It is not Moses who has given you the bread from heaven, but it is My Father who gives you the true bread from heaven. Correction. Moses mediated; God provided. Now the Father gives true bread.
The bread of God is the bread that comes down from heaven and gives life to the world. Definition shift. Bread is a person. Life-giving, universal scope. Jesus is the bread.
Sir, always give us this bread. Misunderstanding. They still think physically. Like the woman at the well wanting perpetual water. Literal thinking misses metaphor.

I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to Me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in Me will never be thirsty. First 'I AM' statement with predicate. Jesus is sustenance itself. Eternal satisfaction in Him.
You have seen Me and still you do not believe. Evidence is not the issue. Seeing does not guarantee believing. The heart, not the eyes, determines faith.
All those the Father gives Me will come to Me, and whoever comes to Me I will never drive away. Divine election and human response. Sovereignty and security. None rejected who come.
I have come down from heaven not to do My will but to do the will of Him who sent Me. Submission to the Father. Mission over preference. Jesus' entire purpose is the Father's will.
This is the will of Him who sent Me: that I shall lose none of all those He has given Me, but raise them up at the last day. Preservation promise. None lost. Future resurrection guaranteed.
Everyone who looks to the Son and believes in Him shall have eternal life, and I will raise them up at the last day. Universal invitation. Belief brings eternal life. Resurrection assured.
The Jews began to grumble about Him because He said, I am the bread that came down from heaven. Offense at the claim. Grumbling echoes wilderness rebellion. They reject His origin.
Is this not Jesus, the son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know? How can He say, I came down from heaven? Familiarity breeds contempt. Known origins blind them to divine identity.
Stop grumbling among yourselves. Command to cease complaining. Murmuring reveals unbelief. Exodus parallel.
No one can come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws them, and I will raise them up at the last day. Divine initiative in salvation. Drawing is sovereign act. Human inability apart from grace.
They will all be taught by God. Isaiah 54:13. Everyone who has heard the Father and learned from Him comes to Me. Divine teaching produces faith. Knowledge of the Father leads to the Son.
No one has seen the Father except the One who is from God; only He has seen the Father. Exclusive access. Jesus alone knows the Father fully. Revelation comes through Him.
The one who believes has eternal life. Present possession. Belief secures life now, not just future. Already/not yet tension.
I am the bread of life. Repetition for emphasis. Identity restated. Jesus is sustenance.
Your ancestors ate the manna in the wilderness, yet they died. Manna was temporary. It sustained physically but could not prevent death. Limitation exposed.
Here is the bread that comes down from heaven, which anyone may eat and not die. Contrast. This bread gives eternal life. Death defeated.

I am the living bread that came down from heaven. Whoever eats this bread will live forever. This bread is My flesh, which I will give for the life of the world. The cross announced. His death is the bread. Atonement imagery. Scandal begins.
The Jews began to argue sharply among themselves. How can this man give us His flesh to eat? Literal interpretation creates offense. Cannibalism implied. They miss metaphor.

Unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink His blood, you have no life in you. Intensification. Drinking blood added. Total identification with His death required. Shocking language deliberate.
Whoever eats My flesh and drinks My blood has eternal life, and I will raise them up at the last day. Consumption equals participation. Union with Christ through His death. Resurrection follows.
My flesh is real food and My blood is real drink. True sustenance. Not metaphor but reality. His death is actual nourishment.
Whoever eats My flesh and drinks My blood remains in Me, and I in them. Mutual indwelling. Abiding through participation. Union achieved through faith in His sacrifice.
As the living Father sent Me and I live because of the Father, so the one who feeds on Me will live because of Me. Chain of life. Father to Son to believer. Derivative life.

This is the bread that came down from heaven. Your ancestors ate manna and died, but whoever feeds on this bread will live forever. Final contrast. Superiority established. Eternal life through Jesus.
He said this while teaching in the synagogue in Capernaum. Public setting. Official religious space. The confrontation is direct.
This is a hard teaching. Who can accept it? Disciples struggle too. Not just opponents. The teaching offends broadly.
Does this offend you? Jesus knows their reaction. He does not soften the message. Offense is intentional.
Then what if you see the Son of Man ascend to where He was before? The ascension will vindicate. Greater sign coming. If this offends, prepare for more.

The Spirit gives life; the flesh counts for nothing. The words I have spoken to you are full of the Spirit and life. Spiritual interpretation required. Literalism fails. His words are Spirit-empowered truth.
Yet there are some of you who do not believe. Jesus knew from the beginning which of them did not believe and who would betray Him. Divine foreknowledge. Unbelief and betrayal foreseen.
This is why I told you that no one can come to Me unless the Father has enabled them. Sovereignty restated. Divine enablement necessary. Human inability absolute.
From this time many of His disciples turned back and no longer followed Him. Mass defection. Hard teaching produces separation. Not everyone who follows stays. True cost of discipleship.
You do not want to leave too, do you? Jesus asks the Twelve. The question tests commitment. Will they also abandon?
Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life. Peter's confession. No alternative. Jesus alone offers life. Commitment through elimination of options.
We have come to believe and to know that You are the Holy One of God. Faith and knowledge combined. Title: Holy One. Set apart, divine. Confession of identity.
Have I not chosen you, the Twelve? Yet one of you is a devil. Even among chosen, betrayal. Judas foreshadowed. Evil infiltrates the inner circle.
He meant Judas, the son of Simon Iscariot, who, though one of the Twelve, was later to betray Him. John's editorial note. The betrayer identified. Proximity to Jesus does not guarantee loyalty.
